speaker: interviewer
text: --Steinweis. And you were saying that now you were-- came to America. Kind of pick it up from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My-- my American family were my-- I still had a grandmother living at that time, my mother's mother, two brothers, and four sisters. And they rented an apartment for us in the Bronx. And my husband, being that he was a tailor, he was able to get a job. And we-- we lived-- we lived a modest life, of course, because we didn't know the-- we didn't know the language. In fact, my husband went to night school to learn whatever he could. And after he came home, he taught me certain things, which are still in my memory. Because me, having a seven-month-old child, wasn't able to go to-- to school. And little by little, we got acquainted with neighbors. And more or less, we tried to find people like us. My sister and my brother still remained in Germany at that time. We were the first to emigrate. And my-- my brother came to this country as a single young man, and he stayed with one of my uncles. In fact, during the Korean War, my brother went into-- my-- my brother served two years in the American army. And my sister arrived with a four-- four-month-old child in the United States. And we all made lives for ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was America very open to you folks--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or was there resentment, or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were-- we were very happy. And the people were-- you know, I'll tell you one thing. Some-- we encountered people who were sometime-- somehow skeptical of our stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, some people were so skeptical, they couldn't believe that horror like that could happen. In fact, ourselves, we-- if we didn't go through it, we wouldn't believe it either. We encountered people who said, how could you live if you didn't-- if you didn't get any food? Of course, we did get food. We-- you have to receive food to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: But some people were very, really, really unbelieving and not receptive to our situation. Some people didn't appreciate the fact that we received helping hands. The Joint Distribution Committee, the HIAS, was helpful to a lot of people. Personally, we didn't, because we had relatives. But a lot of people who arrived, whom we know, friends of ours, were given material things by the Joint Distribution Committee and by-- and by the HIAS. And a lot of the, you know, what they call themselves, old timers, they would say, when I arrived in this country, I worked for $4 a week, and my husband went to work for $40. Of course, $1 an hour, well, you couldn't live very well after taxes. You took out some taxes. But some people-- some people really were not receptive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they the-- were they Jews or non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes, Jews. The funny thing is that-- that some of the Jews were not receptive. But you know, we accepted whatever-- whatever came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And little by little, we tried to-- to better our lives, you know? We moved from one-- from this particular apartment to a better place. And later on, I was able to help out a little with earning some income. Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Doing my sewing-- sewing, yes. And my youngest son was born 1957, 10 years later. And by then, my husband was already able to go into business for himself. He-- we opened a cleaning store in partnership with one of his brothers. And we were doing-- getting better and better. And, you know. And eventually, we were able to buy a home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- when you were here, did you have nightmares about-- about your experience with Germany? Or was it never like that, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: In my particular case, I had-- I can-- I keep things in me. And I was-- my emotional condition caused me, in camp already, to get a lot of digestive problems. In fact, I came-- I had gall bladder gallstones. I-- I had ulcers that are still persisting till now, and for years, and years, and years. And I'm on ulcer medication, and it-- it all has to do with my emotional upset. But nightmares is what my husband gets. Even now, the Germans are chasing him. He wakes up screaming something awful. I have to shake him real hard to-- to-- to wake him out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you-- how do you-- why do you think you don't have that problem?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. I-- I-- I never had-- I never had nightmares.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the experience of going through the Holocaust make you a-- a better, worse, different kind of person than you think you would have been otherwise? I mean, how do you think it's changed you as a person?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I really can't tell you that, because the age difference is such that when you get older, your-- your emotional makeup becomes different. I was young at that time. And of course, returning after the war and finding out that that's all there-- that's all there is-- my sister, myself, and my brother-- after a family of aunts and uncles, and cousins, and grandparents, and-- and that's all there is that-- that remained.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it change-- do you think you're more trusting, less trusting, more-- I mean, what-- did it do anything to you on that level?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I tell you, trusting, I'm not. But I'm afraid. I'm frightened. Every change that occurs frightens me. And sometimes-- sometimes when-- of course, I did have bad dreams that did not pertain to this particular story. But if sometimes, I-- if you want to scream, and you can't, you know? And the same thing applies even in daylight, in daytime life. Sometimes I have-- sometimes-- I had once encountered, like, I thought somebody's going to throw a rock at me. And instead of screaming out, I lost my voice-- so the same thing. It's-- it's that fear of the unknown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the-- going through the Holocaust make you believe more in God, less in God, more religious, the rest? Did it change your view of any of that stuff?

speaker: interviewee
text: Skeptical-- really skeptical. Because I know people who totally lost their-- their faith in God. I'm-- I'm afraid sometimes to express it to people of different persuasions, but I have really become skeptical, that a thing like that should be allowed to happen. And look what's happening in Germany. They came out, such a huge power, such a huge economic power. And-- and-- and-- and-- and the Jews lost some-- so much. So that-- that really makes you stop and think. And-- and you're afraid to think in either direction, so you-- you just stop right there in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: If-- here's a chance for you to say anything you wanna say. And if you want, you know, your grandchildren and great-grandchildren may see this. What do you wanna say? What kind of-- what kind of message do you wanna give to people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, we have to tell them of what happened. We have to continue with our history. In fact, as I told you in our telephone conversation, that my youngest son made it as his profession-- the modern German European history, including the Holocaust studies. And he's doing-- and this is his profession now. And he also wrote a book about the Nazi Germany, the political and economical-- political and economical-- the way that Nazi had influence on the political and economical life in Germany, and about, you know, Jews losing jobs, and being-- everything was driven by the Nazi machine. And so, I'm very happy that the children know that. And they not only know that for themselves, but they are able to relate it to-- for the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, just if you want, is there something you wanna say to future generations, to your grandchildren, great-grandchildren about the Holocaust-- well, anything you want to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want them to know what happened to our people. And I want them to-- to believe that nothing like that ever happens again, you know, with that slogan that has been always spoken for the last 50 years, 'Never again'. I really hope that it never happens again.

speaker: interviewer
text: But who knows? Because there is a Holocaust-- there was a Holocaust going on in-- in Yugoslavia for the last few years. And look what's the-- the anti-Semitic movement in the United States. So there is always, in my background, and always being afraid as a child in an anti-Semitic country, I am always afraid. And I-- I don't suppose I'll ever lose that fear. But I don't want my children to be afraid, and my grandchildren to be afraid of-- of the future as Jews. OK. Let's stop there. I think that's very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Rosalie, who do you have next to you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would like to introduce my husband, David. We have just celebrated our 50th wedding anniversary. And we were very fortunate to have it done with our children, and the grandchildren, and the extended family, his brother's children and grandchildren. And this is the guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anything you wanna say about him? Nice guy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. What I-- what I want to say about him is, like, people always joke that the-- the first year is tough, and that the next 49 are tougher.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But the-- the going has been, you know, up and down.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But we-- we made the best of it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you make a good-looking couple. Thank you, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father, Saul Weisbrot. This picture will probably have been taken 1911, because he was born 1890. And at the age of 21, he had to go into the service of the Russian Czarist Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in-- I wouldn't know where exactly it was taken at. You see, he was serve-- he was serving his three-year service in the Caucasus Mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. About what-- what country are we in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Russia, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Russia, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's take a look at the next picture then. Ready? Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is also a picture of my father. It probably is about the same time, maybe a year less, maybe 20 years or 21-years-old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is probably taken in Kielce, where he lived before entering the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who are we looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is actually an enlargement of a picture of my mother, taken from an original postcard picture. And the reason that I'm able to have pictures of my parents is because my mother's entire family was in the United States. And these pictures were shipped-- mailed to them. And when we arrived in this country, they-- they gave us these pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture must have been taken also before the first World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kielce, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What are we looking at in this picture here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the three of us who actually survived. I am the oldest, my sister's next, and-- and my brother. That time, we must have been, like, five, four, and three, or-- or four, three, and two, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year would this be then?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would be the year, maybe, 1927 or '28.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Kielce, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the names of three people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosalie, Regina, and Harold.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. OK. What are we looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my family. I'll start with my father, going down, my mother; standing next is my brother Harold, who is alive. My mother's holding my sister, Adele. Next to my mother is her sister, Raye, and her son Harold, who came for a visit from the United States. Above that is myself, Rosalie, and my sister Regina. The picture was taken in 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kielce, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. OK. What are we looking at here? Who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my parents. My-- starting from the left is-- his name is Charles Silverberg. He has-- he became my uncle later, after this picture was taken. He married my mother's sister. In the center is my mother, and my father is next to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture must have been taken either 1920 or 1921, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kielce, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are we looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is a picture of my dad, Saul, and my youngest brother, Isaac. It must have been taken either 1940 or 1941. That's about the only picture that I have from during the war. My younger brother played accordion.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the town that we lived just before we were all taken to the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this brother's name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isaac. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and what's this here we're looking at?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a picture of my husband, David's and my wedding. It's not actually taken at the time of our wedding, because they-- there-- there was no way we could have a picture taken at the time. But about-- about a month later, we went to a different town called Plattling, in Germany, where we had the picture taken. The funny thing is that this wedding dress that I am wearing at the wedding, I borrowed from the wife of a Nazi officer, who at this-- at this time, was in-- in the prisoner of war in Russia. And I paid her with a few cans of American Army food rations.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and what year was this picture taken? What date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945. That was-- the wedding was in October. This picture was taken in-- in November.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Deggendorf, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. OK. Who's this handsome couple?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is myself and my husband, shortly after we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In then DP camp at Deggendorf. This camp actually was, before, a military academy. And we each, married couple, had a room that we lived in. And we collected food from a collective kitchen. There was also a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I just need to know the-- the date on this thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945 or beginning '46, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Great.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and what's here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my husband, myself, in 1947. And my son, Saul, is in the carriage. He must be a few months old that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what building are you in front of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in front of the DP camp at Deggendorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. What's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the boat on which we came to the United States. It's an army transport called Ernie Pyle. It was not a large ship. I think it was about 15,000 pound-- ton. And we traveled in November of 1947. And we arrived in this country at a--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Great. Well, thanks. OK. OK, can you introduce the folks in this picture over here? Who do we have?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of-- taken at the time of my youngest son, Alan's, bar mitzvah, in June of 1960. He was born 1957. That would be '60. At the left is my oldest son, Saul. Next is Alan, next, myself, and my husband, David.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in Brooklyn, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. Thanks. Out of sequence, I know, but what are we looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is a picture of my sister Regina and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one's which?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's on the left, and I'm on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was taken 1945. It must have been June or July, in Myslakowice, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right after the war. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are we looking at over here? What's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a picture of my third grade classroom, on the entire student body.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am located, from where I sit now, on the left side, the first row. I'm wearing a dress with a white collar--

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --sitting on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when was this picture taken and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: That has-- that was taken in Kielce. I-- that must have been taken maybe 1930, or probably '30 or '31.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This a picture of my fourth grade. And I'm located at the extreme right, the second from the top row-- second row from the top.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and this picture was taken-- what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This either '30 or '31. I really don't remember-- most likely '31.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kielce.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thanks. Who are we looking at in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture shows my son, Saul, with his wife, Irva, his older daughter, Gayle, who's-- that was the day of her bat mitzvah-- and her younger sister, Melanie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Stony Brook, Long Island.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who are we looking at here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my youngest son, Alan, and his wife, Catherine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken at the time of my-- Gayle's bat mitzvah, 1993, in Stony Brook, Long Island.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.
